Abstract
--------

Many applications of plasmonic nanoparticles require precise control of their optical properties that are governed by nanoparticle dimensions, shape, morphology and composition. Finding reaction conditions for the synthesis of nanoparticles with targeted characteristics is a time-consuming and resource-intensive trial-and-error process, however closed-loop nanoparticle synthesis enables the accelerated exploration of large chemical spaces without human intervention. Here, we introduce the Autonomous Fluidic Identification and Optimization Nanochemistry (AFION) self-driving lab that integrates a microfluidic reactor, in-flow spectroscopic nanoparticle characterization, and machine learning for the exploration and optimization of the multidimensional chemical space for the photochemical synthesis of plasmonic nanoparticles. By targeting spectroscopic nanoparticle properties, the AFION lab identifies reaction conditions for the synthesis of different types of nanoparticles with designated shapes, morphologies, and compositions. Data analysis provides insight into the role of reaction conditions for the synthesis of the targeted nanoparticle type. This work shows that the AFION lab is an effective exploration platform for on-demand synthesis of plasmonic nanoparticles.

Introduction
------------

Applications of inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) in chemical and biological sensing[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR1 "West, J. L. & Halas, N. J. Engineered nanomaterials for biophotonics applications: improving sensing, imaging, and therapeutics. Annu. Rev. Biomed. Eng. 5, 285–292 (2003)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR2 "Nie, Z., Petukhova, A. & Kumacheva, E. Properties and emerging applications of self-assembled structures made from inorganic nanoparticles. Nat. Nanotechnol. 5, 15–25 (2010)."), photovoltaics[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR3 "Burschka, J. et al. Sequential deposition as a route to high-performance perovskite-sensitized solar cells. Nature 499, 316–319 (2013)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR4 "Gao, P., Bin Mohd Yusoff, A. R. & Nazeeruddin, M. K. Dimensionality engineering of hybrid halide perovskite light absorbers. Nat. Commun. 9, 1–14 (2018)."), imaging[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR5 "Popovtzer, R. et al. Targeted gold nanoparticles enable molecular CT imaging of cancer. Nano Lett. 8, 4593–4596 (2008)."), and drug delivery[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR6 "Liong, M. et al. Multifunctional inorganic nanoparticles for imaging, targeting, and drug delivery. ACS Nano 2, 889–896 (2008).") are largely governed by their optical properties. The identification of the multidimensional chemical space for the synthesis of NPs with precisely controlled characteristics is a challenge, since the rate of reagent addition and mixing, reagent concentration, and reaction time and temperature are interdependent and synergistic. A small disturbance in these conditions can significantly change NP properties. Furthermore, with every additional reaction condition, the size of the chemical space exponentially increases[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR7 "Hastie, T., Friedman, J. & Tibshirani, R. The Elements of Statistical Learning. (Springer New York, New York, NY, 2001)."). Thus, the identification of key parameters controlling NP properties through experience-based manual trial-and-error experiments is a time-, labor-, and resource-intensive task.

The use of self-driving labs (SDLs) that operate in a “closed-loop” manner with minimal human intervention emerged as a promising strategy for addressing this challenge. These labs operate via iterative NP syntheses by integrating automation, e.g., robotics or microfluidics (MFs), NP characterization, and machine learning (ML)[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR8 "Abolhasani, M. & Kumacheva, E. The rise of self-driving labs in chemical and materials sciences. Nat. Synth. 2, 483–492 (2023)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR9 "Tao, H. et al. Nanoparticle synthesis assisted by machine learning. Nat. Rev. Mater. 6, 701–716 (2021)."). Automation enables control over reagent injection, mixing, heating, and separation[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR10 "Lohse, S. E., Eller, J. R., Sivapalan, S. T., Plews, M. R. & Murphy, C. J. A simple millifluidic benchtop reactor system for the high-throughput synthesis and functionalization of gold nanoparticles with different sizes and shapes. ACS Nano 7, 4135–4150 (2013)."),[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR11 "Song, Y., Hormes, J. & Kumar, C. S. S. R. Microfluidic synthesis of nanomaterials. Small 4, 698–711 (2008)."). In particular, MFs offers flow-controlled reagent supply, enhanced mass and heat transfer, and real-time online NP characterization[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR12 "Sai Krishna, K. et al. Millifluidics for time-resolved mapping of the growth of gold nanostructures. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 135, 5450–5456 (2013)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR13 "Długosz, O. & Banach, M. Despite the advantages of automation, the decision on the next-step syntheses for identification of the most effective NP reaction conditions remains in the hands of the operator. For instance, in the SDL performing seeded synthesis of gold (Au) nanospheres, rods, and octahedrons, an evolutionary genetic algorithm was used to optimize a fitness function for the selected areas under the plasmon peak[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56788-9#ref-CR28 "Salley, D. et al. A nanomaterials discovery robot for the Darwinian evolution of shape programmable gold nanoparticles. Nat. Commun. 11, 2771 (2020)."). Self‐driven multistep quantum dot synthesis enabled by autonomous robotic experimentation in flow. Adv. Intell. Syst. 3, 2000245 (2021)."). D. et al. Algorithms for the self-optimisation of chemical reactions. React. Chem. Eng. 4, 1545–1554 (2019)."). The use of a hierarchical algorithm would obviate the need to assign appropriate individual weights to each objective and would focus on their hierarchical importance. More specifically, the secondary objective would not be fulfilled if it causes a degradation in the objective that is higher in the hierarchy. # Chemical synthesis of novel plasmonic nanoparticles

## Abstract

Under the irradiation of light, the free electrons in a plasmonic nanoparticle are driven by the alternating electric field to collectively oscillate at a resonant frequency in a phenomenon known as surface plasmon resonance. Both calculations and measurements have shown that the frequency and amplitude of the resonance are sensitive to particle shape, which determines how the free electrons are polarized and distributed on the surface. As a result, controlling the shape of a plasmonic nanoparticle represents the most powerful means of tailoring and fine-tuning its optical resonance properties. In a solution-phase synthesis, the shape displayed by a nanoparticle is determined by the crystalline structure of the initial seed produced and the interaction of different seed facets with capping agents. Using polyol synthesis as a typical example, we illustrate how oxidative etching and kinetic control can be employed to manipulate the shapes and optical responses of plasmonic nanoparticles made of either Ag or Pd. We conclude by highlighting a few fundamental studies and applications enabled by plasmonic nanoparticles having well-defined and controllable shapes.

### 1 Plasmonic Nanoparticles

The starting material for an [SERS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/raman-spectra "Learn more about SERS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tag is a plasmonic [nanoparticle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Noble metal [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") support localized [surface plasmon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-plasmon "Learn more about surface plasmon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") resonances (LSPRs), a collective oscillation of electrons that strongly couple to light at specific wavelengths, and produce extremely high [electromagnetic fields](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electromagnetic-field "Learn more about electromagnetic fields from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") near the nanoparticle surface.

In bulk measurements, the LSPR results in distinctive changes in the extinction of a nanoparticle suspension. The extinction is the result of increases in both nanoparticle absorbance and scatter, and the wavelengths at which plasmonic nanoparticles interact with light can be tuned by varying the [composition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/phase-composition "Learn more about composition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), shape, and size of the particle (Fig. 3). Gold and [silver](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/silver "Learn more about silver from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") nanoparticles are the most commonly used plasmonic nanoparticles as they absorb and scatter light in the 350–900 nm spectral range. For example, solid silver spheres exhibit a resonance in the blue, shifting to longer wavelengths as the diameter increases. Solid gold spheres have resonances at green wavelengths, also red shifting as the diameter increases. Thin shells of silver or gold over a nonconducting material such as silica also support an LSPR that can lead to strong SERS enhancements (Oldenburg _et al._, 1999). In this case, the resonance wavelength depends on the ratio of the shell thickness to the core diameter. Asymmetric structures such as [nanorods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanorod "Learn more about nanorods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and spheroids support strong resonances on their long axes, the wavelength of which varies with the particle's aspect ratio (Hao and Schatz, 2004; Murphy _et al._, 2008; Orendorff _et al._, 2006). Beyond spheres, shells, and rods, a variety of other nanoparticle structures have been shown to produce SERS including nanocubes (McLellan _et al._, 2006), nanostars (Khoury and Vo-Dinh, 2008), [nanoflowers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoflower "Learn more about nanoflowers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Xie _et al._, 2008), and nanorice (Wiley _et al._, 2007).

Fig. 3. Wavelength tunability of plasmonic nanoparticles. Gold and silver nanostructures have size- and shape-dependent optical properties that can be predicted from electromagnetic theory or numerical simulations. (A) Solid spheres of silver and gold have resonance wavelengths that shift to longer wavelengths with the sphere radius. (B) Gold nanorod resonance wavelengths shift to longer wavelengths with aspect ratio. (C) Shells of gold over a silica core have resonance wavelengths that shift to longer wavelengths as the ratio of core radius to shell thickness increases.

The electric field intensity resulting from the LSPR varies across the surface of the nanoparticle, and depends on the shape of the nanoparticle, its orientation relative to the exciting light, and the polarization and wavelength of that light. The LSPR is predicted to be highest at particle edges, points, or surface irregularities, which has led to the concept of SERS “hot spots” as being responsible for generating the highest SERS enhancement and signals. Additionally, the LSPRs of adjacent nanoparticles can couple, creating hotspots at the junctions between aggregated particles (Camden _et al._, 2008). The engineering of metal nanoparticle systems to provide the highest and most uniform LSPRs and SERS enhancement is a key goal of SERS tag design.

### 5 Conclusions

In this article we described the applications of various [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that can be used for [photodynamic therapy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/photodynamic-therapy "Learn more about photodynamic therapy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PDT), and possible methods of detecting the [singlet oxygen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/singlet-oxygen "Learn more about singlet oxygen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generated. Plasmonic nanoparticles exhibit a number of desirable properties for use in [PDT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/photodynamic-therapy "Learn more about PDT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Firstly, they are able to generate singlet oxygen during irradiation with light in a range between 800 and 1000 nm, where human tissue exhibits a transparency window. In comparison with typical organic [photosensitizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photosensitizer "Learn more about photosensitizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as [hematoporphyrins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/hematoporphyrin "Learn more about hematoporphyrins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), they exhibit higher photostability and resistance to enzymatic degradation. Also, their higher excitation coefficients make a reduction in the power of the excitation light possible. [Upconversion nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/upconversion-nanoparticle "Learn more about Upconversion nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") also make excitation by NIR light possible, and emissions at shorter wavelengths that are compatible with organic photosensitizers. [Quantum dots](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/quantum-dot "Learn more about Quantum dots from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are another possibility for use in PDT, although at present they have less than optimal properties for clinical applications. Their main disadvantages are the presence of heavy metals, which are potentially toxic, and an [absorption band](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/absorption-spectra "Learn more about absorption band from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the visual region of the spectrum (400–500 nm), where penetration of light through tissues is not high. It is expected that, due to their very promising [optical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-property "Learn more about optical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), plasmonic nanoparticles, plasmonic composites, and upconversion [nanoconjugates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanoconjugate "Learn more about nanoconjugates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will have a significant impact on the detection and treatment of cancer in the near future.

### 6.2.0 Plasmonic Nanostructured Materials

[Plasmonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/plasmonics "Learn more about Plasmonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") structures such as [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), nanoposts, and [nanotubes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanotube "Learn more about nanotubes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of noble metal can be used for generation of [plasmonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/plasmonics "Learn more about plasmonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") signals. Plasmonic [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including nanoparticles, nanotubes, etc. can be used to minimize internal losses such as the formation of energetic charge carriers and can maximize the light confinement by taking advantage of the charge carrier formation and transfer processes.

[Metallic nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metal-nanoparticle "Learn more about Metallic nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of gold (Au), platinum (Pt), and silver (Ag) exhibit high absorption of light and scattering behavior of light. Plasmonic optical responses of these metallic nanoparticles can be tuned by changing the nanoparticles' shape, size, and composition through visible to near-infrared regions of the EM spectrum. Also, the color of dispersed plasmonic nanoparticles or films can be altered by shifting the absorption and scattering. Au and Ag nanoparticles show unique [spectral responses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/spectral-response "Learn more about spectral responses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") due to their specific wavelengths of light that can drive the conduction electrons to collectively oscillate. At resonances of these plasmonic nanoparticles, the absorption and scattering intensities can be found up to×40 higher than the nonplasmonic particles. The tunability and brightness of these nanoparticles make them highly useful for molecule tracking, imaging, and detection. Mainly two prominent approaches are widely used for synthesis of plasmonic nanostructures:

▪
bottom–up chemical approach

▪
top–down approach.

The bottom–up chemical approach produces the large-scale synthesis of plasmonic nanostructures with a variety of shapes and sizes. These plasmonic nanostructures can be further modified with functional molecules to selectively bind with desired biorecognition molecules that can either be assembled on plasmonic nanobiochips or be injected into cells up to the detection intracellular concentration of several biomarkers. There are several [chemical methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/chemical-processing "Learn more about chemical methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that can be employed for synthesis of Au nanostructures such as nanoparticles, [nanorods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nanorod "Learn more about nanorods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), nanotubes, nanodisks, etc. This can be done by the reduction of Au salt in the presence of a [surfactant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-active-agent "Learn more about surfactant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For example, 12- to 50-nm diameter spherical Au nanoparticles (Fig.6.1.2A and B) were synthesized via [sodium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/sodium "Learn more about sodium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") citrate reduction of chloroauric acid (HAuCl 4) wherein the shape of the nanoparticles was tuned by varying the[molar ratio](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/molar-ratio "Learn more about molar ratio from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of sodium citrate and tetrachloroauric acid [12]. Recently, Tadepalli et al. synthesized Au nanorods via the seed-mediated approach with a length of 47.3±2.3 nm and a diameter of 20.2±1.4 nm for detection of cardiac biomarkers (Fig.6.1.2C) [13]. Fig.6.1.3 demonstrates several methods for preparation of metal nanostructures.

The top–down approach including lithographic methods such as [photolithography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-lithography "Learn more about photolithography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [soft lithography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/soft-lithography "Learn more about soft lithography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), electron-beam lithography, nanoimprinting lithography, etc. allows fabrication of ordered and periodic plasmonic nanostructures. This approach offers more reproducible [structural properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/structural-property "Learn more about structural properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of plasmonic nanostructures, thus the [optical property](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-property "Learn more about optical property from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be tuned by the input parameters during their fabrication process. Nano- or [microfabrication](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microfabrication "Learn more about microfabrication from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the collection of techniques that produces reproducible patterns with a feature size of 100 nm or less in at least one dimension. Several lithographic methods were employed to create plasmonic structures for the development of [diagnostic devices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/diagnostic-device "Learn more about diagnostic devices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Various fields including nanoscience have the potential demand for new applications in plasmonics and health care devices.

A simple photolithography technique can also be used to create plasmonic nanostructures. It uses light to transfer a geometric pattern from an optical photomask to a photoresist (light sensitive) on a desired substrate. However, soft photolithography is simple, and it is easier to create plasmonic nanostructures using soft photolithography than conventional photolithography. The soft lithography technique replicates nanostructures using elastomeric stamps, molds, and conformable photomasks. It is called “soft” because it uses elastomeric materials such as [polydimethylsiloxane](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/polydimethylsiloxane "Learn more about polydimethylsiloxane from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PDMS). The potential benefits of soft photolithography are given below:

▪
lower cost and cheaper mass production

▪
well suited for plasmonic nanostructures in [biomedical fields](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomedical-field "Learn more about biomedical fields from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")

▪
patterns can be more easily transferred than in traditional lithography techniques

▪
photoreactive surface is not required to create a [nanostructure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")

▪
smaller feature size of nanostructures than photolithography (∼30 nm vs. ∼100 nm).

▪
resolution of up to 6 nm can be achieved

A schematic representation of ultraviolet (UV) [nanoimprint lithography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nanoimprint-lithography "Learn more about nanoimprint lithography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process is shown in Fig.6.1.4[14]. In this process, first a UV-sensitive resist is used to spin coat on a transparent mold containing nanostructures on its surface. Then, a UV transparent stamp is deposited on a desired substrate at low pressure (∼0 and 1 bar) and at room temperature (25°C), and the soft stamp is released from the substrate. Then, the residual layer of the UV-sensitive resist from the printed opposite nanopattern of the transparent mold is removed. After this the reactive-ion etching process is performed to get the desired patterns on the UV resist [14]. The main advantages of this process are the transparent flexible stamp, and excellent pattern homogeneity. The UV flexible stamp fabrication can be mainly realized by using PDMS as it offers excellent chemical, [mechanical stability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mechanical-stability "Learn more about mechanical stability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and high [optical transparency](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-transparency "Learn more about optical transparency from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[15].

Another example of soft nanoimprint lithography technique for creating plasmonic array consisting of nanoposts was given by Truong et al. [16]. The fabrication of Au nanopost-based plasmonic requires several consecutive molding steps. In this fabrication, a master comprising photoresists (PRs) and is a square array of nanowells of diameter ∼480 nm, periodicity of ∼780 nm, and relief depth of ∼350 nm was pattered on a [Si substrate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/si-substrate "Learn more about Si substrate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[16]. To create nanoposts: acryloxy perfluoropolyether (a-PFPE) was drop cast and cured under [UV light](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ultraviolet-light "Learn more about UV light from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with a [polyethylene terephthalate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polyethylene-terephthalate "Learn more about polyethylene terephthalate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PET) backing support resulting in an a-PFPE/PET composite mold. Casting and curing of hard PDMS (h-PDMS) and soft-PDMS (s-PDMS) against the a-PFPE/PET mold create an h-PDMS/s-PDMS composite mold containing nanowells. Furthermore, molding a UV-curable [polyurethane](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polyurethane "Learn more about polyurethane from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PU) with the h-PDMS/s-PDMS composite mold forms the PU replica with an array of nanoposts. Alternately for nanowells the casting and curing of h-PDMS and s-PDMS against the original master generates the h-PDMS/s-PDMS mold with nanoposts. Finally, molding PU with the h-PDMS/s-PDMS mold under UV light can form nanowells. Thick layers of Au on both structures can further form nanoposts and nanowells-based plasmonic crystals [16]. The soft lithographic technique consists of a number of techniques such as soft elastomeric stamps, molds, and conformable photomasks. This method is mostly applied in the research field of [photonics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/photonics "Learn more about photonics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and biotechnology, microfluidics, and electronics. This method offers flexible patterning capabilities and experimental simplicity, is easy to use, and is of low cost [17]. Au nanodisk array-based plasmonic biosensing chip was fabricated using the bottom–up [nanofabrication](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nanofabrication-process "Learn more about nanofabrication from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technique with hole–mask colloidal lithography [18]. Unlike soft nanoimprinting lithography, the electron-beam lithography scans a focused beam of electrons to draw desired shapes on a surface covered by electron-sensitive resist. The e-beam can alter solubility of the resist, allowing selective removal of exposed regions or nonexposed regions of the resist by immersing it in a solvent. This is a high resolution, low throughput, and [maskless lithography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/maskless-lithography "Learn more about maskless lithography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and it can be utilized to draw customized patterns (DirectWrite) with sub-10 nm resolution. Koh et al. demonstrated the use of high-resolution e-beam lithography to fabricate nanocavities with nanometric spatial and positional control (Fig.6.1.7) [19].

enriched_points.expanded_content: This method involves reducing metal salts, like gold or silver, to form nanoparticles, utilizing reducing agents such as sodium citrate or borohydride, which effectively create stable plasmonic nanostructures.

Chemical synthesis offers versatile approaches for producing a variety of nanoparticles, including spherical, rod-shaped, and triangular forms, each with unique optical properties valuable in applications.

By manipulating reaction conditions such as temperature and reagent concentration, researchers can engineer nanoparticles with precise sizes and shapes, which directly influence their optical resonance characteristics.

Advancements in synthesis techniques, including microfluidics and self-driving labs, significantly simplify the nanoparticle production process, enhancing efficiency and reproducibility while reducing labor and resource consumption.
